title: Lung Cancer — SCLC
oneLiner: Rapidly growing neuroendocrine lung cancer strongly linked to smoking; presents with central mass, paraneoplastic syndromes, and early metastatic spread.

sections.Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC): **One-liner:** Rapidly growing neuroendocrine lung cancer strongly linked to smoking; presents with central mass, paraneoplastic syndromes, and early metastatic spread.
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**
A 62-year-old man with a 45-pack-year smoking history presents with a 6-week history of progressive cough, hemoptysis, and wheezing. He also reports recent onset of facial swelling and distended neck veins. On examination, he has reduced breath sounds on the right, a palpable right supraclavicular lymph node, and mild ptosis with miosis on the left side. His chest X-ray shows a large right hilar mass with mediastinal widening.

**Dissecting the Vignette — Pattern Clues:**
- **Heavy smoker, older male:** Classic demographic – SCLC is almost exclusively in smokers.
- **Cough, hemoptysis, wheeze:** Central airway involvement – SCLC typically arises in major bronchi.
- **Facial swelling, distended neck veins:** Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO) – a common presentation due to rapid mediastinal growth.
- **Reduced breath sounds, supraclavicular lymph node:** Local extension and lymphatic spread.
- **Ptosis, miosis (Horner's syndrome):** Apical lung involvement (Pancoast variant) – rare, but SCLC can infiltrate the sympathetic chain.
- **Chest X-ray: large hilar mass + mediastinal widening:** Classic radiological pattern – central mass, hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

**Atypical Presentations That Trick You:**
1. **Neurological first:** Patient presents with limb weakness, ataxia, or diplopia. Think Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) or cerebellar degeneration before you find the lung mass. Use anti-neuronal antibodies (anti-Hu, anti-Yo). Imaging may still show only a small primary.
2. **SIADH without cancer:** Hyponatremia, confusion, seizures. Workup reveals elevated ADH, chest CT shows a tiny hilar nodule. Don't blame other causes until you've excluded SCLC.
3. **Back pain + leg weakness:** Spinal cord compression from vertebral metastases. The primary may be silent. SCLC is the lung cancer that most commonly presents this way.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Small cell lung cancer is a high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma of the lung, characterized by small, oat-shaped cells with scant cytoplasm, rapid doubling time (often <30 days), and early systemic dissemination. It accounts for ~15% of all lung cancers.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** 2nd most common lung cancer after adenocarcinoma; ~30,000 new cases/year in the US.
- **Stakes:** Extremely aggressive. Without treatment, median survival is 2–4 months. Even with treatment, 5-year survival for limited-stage is ~20–25%, for extensive-stage <5%.
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosing as non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) on small biopsies (look for neuroendocrine markers like synaptophysin, chromogranin, TTF-1). Missing paraneoplastic syndromes as first clues. Underestimating the urgency – you must act fast.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of Development:**
It starts in the bronchial mucosa, likely from neuroendocrine cells (Kulchitsky cells). Chronic tobacco carcinogens (benzo[a]pyrene, nitrosamines) cause DNA damage. Key mutations: loss of tumor suppressor genes (TP53, RB1) and activation of oncogenes (MYC, BCL2). The cells dedifferentiate into small, round, blue cells that proliferate rapidly. They are exquisitely sensitive to chemotherapy and radiation, but resistance almost always develops.

**Why Each Key Sign Happens (Mechanism → Sign):**
- **Central mass → cough, hemoptysis, wheeze:** The tumor arises near the carina or main bronchi, obstructing airflow and eroding blood vessels.
- **Mediastinal invasion → SVCO:** Tumor infiltrates the SVC wall, causing upstream venous congestion. This is an oncologic emergency.
- **Paraneoplastic syndromes:**
  - **SIADH (hyponatremia):** Tumor secretes ectopic ADH (vasopressin) – 15% of SCLC patients.
  - **Cushing's syndrome (ectopic ACTH):** Paraneoplastic overproduction of ACTH causes hypokalemia, hypertension, hyperglycemia. Rare but distinctive.
  - **LEMS (weakness, autonomic dysfunction):** Autoantibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels at neuromuscular junction. Improves with treatment? Yes, SCLC treatment can help.
- **Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis):** Apical (Pancoast) involvement destroys the sympathetic chain.
- **Rapid growth and early metastasis:** Angiogenesis and hematogenous spread. Common sites: brain (30% at diagnosis – that's why MRI brain is routine), liver, bone, adrenal glands.

**Connect to Pattern Recognition:**
The classic vignette combines local effects (cough, SVCO) with paraneoplastic (none in that vignette, but add LEMS if legs weak) and rapid progression (6 weeks, not months). The atypical presentations (LEMS, SIADH) are direct consequences of the neuroendocrine nature.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause / Trigger | 1-line stem clue that points to it |
|----------|-----------------|------------------------------------|
| **Environmental** | Cigarette smoking (90+%) | "Heavy smoker, 40+ pack-years" |
| | Occupational exposure (asbestos, radon, diesel exhaust) | "Worked in shipyard / uranium mine" |
| | Second-hand smoke (rare, but relevant) | "Non-smoker, but husband smoked" |
| **Idiopathic** | Unknown – SCLC in never-smoker without known exposure | "No smoking history, no exposures" – very rare; consider genetic predisposition (e.g., TP53 germline mutation) |
| **Immunosuppression** | HIV / organ transplant (increased risk of all lung cancers) | "On tacrolimus / HIV positive" |
| **Genetic** | Family history – first-degree relative with lung cancer | "Father died of lung cancer aged 50" |

**Most common cause:** Cigarette smoking. Over 90% of SCLC cases occur in smokers. This is the strongest risk factor for any lung cancer. Document pack-years.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Steps (with staging):**
1. **Chest X-ray (CXR)** – Often first clue. Shows hilar mass, mediastinal widening, maybe atelectasis.
   - *What it shows:* Central opacity.
   - *Why order:* Cheap, fast, but limited.
2. **CT chest + abdomen (with contrast)** – Defines primary tumor, mediastinal nodes, distant metastases (liver, adrenal).
   - *What it shows:* Enhancing central mass, often with bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
   - *Why:* Staging – we need to know limited vs. extensive stage.
3. **Biopsy** – Needle aspiration or bronchoscopy with endobronchial biopsy.
   - *What it shows:* Small blue cells, scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, high mitotic rate.
   - *Immunohistochemistry:* Synaptophysin +, chromogranin +, TTF-1 +, CD56 +, Ki-67 >50%.
   - *Why:* Confirm diagnosis and distinguish from NSCLC, lymphoma, carcinoid.
4. **MRI brain (with contrast)** – Mandatory for all SCLC (even if asymptomatic) due to high brain metastasis risk.
   - *What it shows:* Enhancing brain lesions – often multiple.
5. **PET-CT (or bone scan + CT)** – For staging when limited stage suspected.
   - *What it shows:* FDG-avid primary, nodes, metastases.
6. **Bloods:**
   - **FBC, LFT, LDH** – LDH is a prognostic marker (high LDH → worse prognosis).
   - **Serum sodium** – Check for SIADH. Normal is 135-145; <130 is moderate; <120 is severe.
   - **Cortisol / ACTH** if suspect ectopic Cushing.
7. **Pulmonary function tests (PFTs)** – Before treatment planning, especially if curative-intent chemoradiotherapy.

**Pattern Recognition – Single Finding That Confirms/Rules Out:**
- *Confirms:* Biopsy + neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin positive, TTF-1 positive) + high Ki-67.
- *Rules out:* If biopsy shows gland formation, squamous pearls – think NSCLC. If cells are large with prominent nucleoli – think large cell carcinoma. If lymphocyte-rich and low Ki-67 – think carcinoid.

**Staging System:**
SCLC uses a two-tier system (not TNM routinely): **Limited stage (LS)** = tumor confined to one hemithorax + ipsilateral mediastinal/hilar nodes, can be encompassed by a single radiotherapy port. **Extensive stage (ES)** = disease beyond that (contralateral lung, pleural effusion, distant metastases). About 30% present limited, 70% extensive.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – Oncologic Emergencies:**
- **Superior vena cava obstruction:** Elevate head, IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat (reduce edema), call oncology for urgent radiotherapy or stenting. Do not delay biopsy – it's safe.
- **Spinal cord compression:** Dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat, urgent MRI spine, consult neurosurgery / oncology. Radiotherapy 2-3 fractions.
- **Severe hyponatremia (SIADH):** Sodium <120 with seizures → hypertonic saline (3%) slow infusion (1-2 mL/kg over 1-2h), aim for 4-6 mmol rise. Furosemide if fluid overloaded. Then switch to tolvaptan (oral V2 antagonist) or demeclocycline.
- **Brain metastases with raised ICP:** Dexamethasone 8-16 mg IV daily, urgent whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) or stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) if few lesions.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – Definitive Treatment Planning:**
- **Limited stage (LS-SCLC):**
  - Concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CRT) – platinum + etoposide (cisplatin 75 mg/m² IV day 1 + etoposide 100 mg/m² IV days 1-3, q21d x4 cycles) + thoracic radiotherapy (≥1.5 Gy b.i.d. to total 45 Gy, or 66 Gy once daily). Start ASAP.
  - Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) – after CRT, if good response. Reduces brain mets risk but may impair cognition.
- **Extensive stage (ES-SCLC):**
  - Chemotherapy: carboplatin (AUC 5-6 IV day 1) + etoposide (100 mg/m² IV days 1-3) q21d x4-6 cycles.
  - Immunotherapy: Add atezolizumab (1200 mg IV q3w) or durvalumab (1500 mg IV q3w) – now standard for ES-SCLC. Maintenance with immune checkpoint inhibitor after chemo.
  - PCI not routinely recommended for ES-SCLC due to lack of survival benefit.

**Long-term:**
- Follow-up with CT chest every 3-6 months for 2 years, then annually.
- Manage treatment side effects: neutropenic fever (G-CSF), esophagitis (radiotherapy), fatigue, nausea.
- Palliative care: for progressive disease, second-line chemotherapy (topotecan, lurbinectedin) or clinical trials.
- Smoking cessation – improves outcomes and reduces second primary cancers.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT delay biopsy for SVCO – stenting/RT can proceed post-biopsy.
- Do NOT give whole-brain RT for 1-2 small brain mets – use SRS if possible.
- Do NOT use anti-VEGF (bevacizumab) in SCLC – increased risk of hemorrhage and no proven benefit.
- Do NOT forget PCI in LS-SCLC with complete response – it saves lives.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Small Cell Lung Cancer | Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) | Carcinoid Tumor | Lymphoma (Mediastinal) |
|---------|-----------------------|------------------------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Smoking** | Strong link (90%+ smokers) | Modest link (adenocarcinoma can be in never-smokers) | Weak/no link | None |
| **Growth rate** | Rapid (weeks) | Slow to moderate (months) | Very slow (years) | Moderate (weeks-months) |
| **Paraneoplastic syndromes** | Very common (SIADH, LEMS, ACTH) | Less common (hypercalcemia, HOA) | Rare (carcinoid syndrome if liver mets – very rare for lung) | Rare (fever, night sweats, pruritus) |
| **Central vs peripheral** | Central (major bronchi) | Variable (squamous: central; adeno: peripheral) | Central (usually) | Mediastinal (anterior) |
| **Biopsy histology** | Small blue cells, scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding | Large cells, gland formation/squamous pearls | Organoid nests, low mitotic rate | Lymphocytes with germinal centers |
| **Immunohistochemistry** | Synaptophysin +, chromogranin +, TTF-1 +, Ki-67 >50% | Cytokeratin +, p40 (squamous), TTF-1 (adeno) | Synaptophysin +, chromogranin +, Ki-67 <20% | CD45 +, B-cell or T-cell markers |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Rapid progression + paraneoplastic + heavy smoking | Larger tumor, no paraneoplastic, slower course | Incidental finding, no smoking, indolent | B symptoms (fever, weight loss) + bulky mediastinal nodes |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major Complications:**

1. **Superior Vena Cava Obstruction (SVCO)** – Presents with facial/neck swelling, dyspnea, distended neck veins, stridor. Key signs: Pemberton's sign (facial congestion on raising arms). Action: urgent dexamethasone + radiotherapy/stent. If missed: airway compromise, cerebral edema, death.
2. **Spinal Cord Compression** – Present with back pain (often first), then leg weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction. Key signs: percussion tenderness over vertebra. Action: high-dose dexamethasone, MRI urgent, radiotherapy or surgical decompression. If missed: permanent paraplegia.
3. **Hyponatremia (SIADH)** – Nausea, confusion, seizures, coma. Key signs: euvolemic hyponatremia, high urine osmolality (>100 mOsm/kg), low serum osmolality. Action: fluid restriction, 3% saline if severe, tolvaptan. If untreated: osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis) if corrected too fast.
4. **Brain Metastases** – Headache, focal deficits, seizure, cognitive decline. Key signs: papill edema on fundoscopy. Action: MRI brain, corticosteroids, WBRT or SRS. If missed: herniation, death.
5. **Venous Thromboembolism (VTE)** – SCLC is highly prothrombotic. DVT/PE can be first presentation. Action: anticoagulation (low molecular weight heparin preferred). If missed: fatal PE.
6. **Radiation Esophagitis** – After CRT: painful swallowing, odynophagia. Key sign: dysphagia. Action: oral analgesics, liquid diet, PPI. If severe: NG tube.
7. **Neutropenic Sepsis** – Post-chemotherapy: fever, rigors, source often unknown. Action: urgent blood cultures, IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h), G-CSF.

**Late Presentation Patterns (unmissable):**
"The patient presents with bilateral leg weakness and urinary retention. He has a 50 pack-year history. Chest X-ray shows a hilar mass. What is the most likely cause?" -> Spinal cord compression from SCLC metastasis.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **"SMALL CELL" for presentation clues:**
   - **S**moking history (almost always)
   - **M**ediastinal mass (central)
   - **A**rrhythmias? no – but **A**utonomic symptoms (LEMS: constipation, dry mouth)
   - **L**abial swelling (facial/SVCO)
   - **L**imp limb (brain mets or cord compression)
   - **C**ough, hemoptysis
   - **E**ndocrine (SIADH, ACTH)
   - **L**ow sodium
   - **L**DH elevated

2. **"EDGE" for staging:**
   - **E**ncompassable in one radiotherapy port? (Limited stage)
   - **D**istant metastasis? (Extensive stage)
   - **G**rade: high (Ki-67 >50%)
   - **E**mboli? (VTE risk)

3. **"VIP" for treatment drugs (historical, but memorable):**
   - **V**P-16 (etoposide)
   - **I**phosphamide (second-line)
   - **P**latinum (cisplatin/carboplatin)

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls (Pattern Recognition Cues):**

1. **Think SCLC first in a heavy smoker with a central lung mass and any paraneoplastic syndrome.** The differential is narrow.
2. **Hyponatremia in a smoker?** Check chest CT and urine osmolality. SCLC causes SIADH in 15%. Don't blame other causes until proven.
3. **Proximal muscle weakness + 'getting better' with repeated movement?** Think Lambert-Eaton, not myasthenia. EMG shows incrementing response. Anti-VGCC antibodies.
4. **Mediastinal widening on CXR?** SCLC, lymphoma, or sarcoid. The smoking history points to SCLC.
5. **Single brain lesion in a smoker?** Almost always SCLC metastasis. Lesion likely contrast-enhancing, peritumoral edema.
6. **Pitting edema of the face?** That's SVCO. Test for distended neck veins. It's an emergency.
7. **Rapidly growing mass in a smoker: SCLC doubles size in <30 days.** If you see a mass that's grown from 2 to 4 cm in a month, it's SCLC until proven otherwise.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**
- **Calling it NSCLC on small biopsy** – always stain for neuroendocrine markers in poorly differentiated lung cancer.
- **Not ordering an MRI brain** – SCLC metastasizes to brain early. MRI is part of staging.
- **Using CT alone for mediastinal staging** – PET-CT or endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) is better.
- **Withholding prophylactic cranial irradiation for fear of cognitive decline** – in LS-SCLC with good response, PCI reduces brain mets and improves survival.
- **Giving whole-brain RT for single small metastasis** – consider stereotactic radiosurgery first.
- **Underestimating the urgency** – SCLC is fast. Start workup ASAP.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (Registrar / Consultant):**
- **SVCO with stridor or respiratory distress** – risk of airway compromise. Call oncology, consider stenting. Start dexamethasone.
- **SiADH with seizures or GCS <15** – call ITU, start 3% saline.
- **Spinal cord compression** – call neurosurgery and oncology for urgent radiotherapy.
- **New neurological symptoms (headache, focal deficits)** – arrange urgent CT head / MRI brain.
- **Febrile neutropenia** – if neutrophils <1.0 and temp >38°C, call oncology and start IV antibiotics. Consider sepsis protocol.
- **Massive hemoptysis** – call for rigid bronchoscopy / embolization.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (or any single parameter score 3) – escalate to critical care outreach.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** – suggests tissue hypoperfusion; ICU review.
- **GCS ≤ 12** (or drop by ≥2) – call anaesthetics for airway assessment, CT head.
- **Sodium <125 mmol/L** (or <120 with symptoms) – consider ITU admission for controlled correction.
- **Platelets <20,000/mcL** or **active bleeding** – call haematology and transfuse.
- **Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) not responding to fluids** – sepsis or PE? Call ICU.

**When to call crash team:** Cardiorespiratory arrest, witnessed seizure with loss of airway, unresponsive patient with agonal breathing.

---
*End of study-mode entry. Build your knowledge bank with this template. Revise by writing your own vignettes for each complication.*

rawMarkdown:
## Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC)
**One-liner:** Rapidly growing neuroendocrine lung cancer strongly linked to smoking; presents with central mass, paraneoplastic syndromes, and early metastatic spread.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**
A 62-year-old man with a 45-pack-year smoking history presents with a 6-week history of progressive cough, hemoptysis, and wheezing. He also reports recent onset of facial swelling and distended neck veins. On examination, he has reduced breath sounds on the right, a palpable right supraclavicular lymph node, and mild ptosis with miosis on the left side. His chest X-ray shows a large right hilar mass with mediastinal widening.

**Dissecting the Vignette — Pattern Clues:**
- **Heavy smoker, older male:** Classic demographic – SCLC is almost exclusively in smokers.
- **Cough, hemoptysis, wheeze:** Central airway involvement – SCLC typically arises in major bronchi.
- **Facial swelling, distended neck veins:** Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO) – a common presentation due to rapid mediastinal growth.
- **Reduced breath sounds, supraclavicular lymph node:** Local extension and lymphatic spread.
- **Ptosis, miosis (Horner's syndrome):** Apical lung involvement (Pancoast variant) – rare, but SCLC can infiltrate the sympathetic chain.
- **Chest X-ray: large hilar mass + mediastinal widening:** Classic radiological pattern – central mass, hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

**Atypical Presentations That Trick You:**
1. **Neurological first:** Patient presents with limb weakness, ataxia, or diplopia. Think Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) or cerebellar degeneration before you find the lung mass. Use anti-neuronal antibodies (anti-Hu, anti-Yo). Imaging may still show only a small primary.
2. **SIADH without cancer:** Hyponatremia, confusion, seizures. Workup reveals elevated ADH, chest CT shows a tiny hilar nodule. Don't blame other causes until you've excluded SCLC.
3. **Back pain + leg weakness:** Spinal cord compression from vertebral metastases. The primary may be silent. SCLC is the lung cancer that most commonly presents this way.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Small cell lung cancer is a high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma of the lung, characterized by small, oat-shaped cells with scant cytoplasm, rapid doubling time (often <30 days), and early systemic dissemination. It accounts for ~15% of all lung cancers.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** 2nd most common lung cancer after adenocarcinoma; ~30,000 new cases/year in the US.
- **Stakes:** Extremely aggressive. Without treatment, median survival is 2–4 months. Even with treatment, 5-year survival for limited-stage is ~20–25%, for extensive-stage <5%.
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosing as non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) on small biopsies (look for neuroendocrine markers like synaptophysin, chromogranin, TTF-1). Missing paraneoplastic syndromes as first clues. Underestimating the urgency – you must act fast.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of Development:**
It starts in the bronchial mucosa, likely from neuroendocrine cells (Kulchitsky cells). Chronic tobacco carcinogens (benzo[a]pyrene, nitrosamines) cause DNA damage. Key mutations: loss of tumor suppressor genes (TP53, RB1) and activation of oncogenes (MYC, BCL2). The cells dedifferentiate into small, round, blue cells that proliferate rapidly. They are exquisitely sensitive to chemotherapy and radiation, but resistance almost always develops.

**Why Each Key Sign Happens (Mechanism → Sign):**
- **Central mass → cough, hemoptysis, wheeze:** The tumor arises near the carina or main bronchi, obstructing airflow and eroding blood vessels.
- **Mediastinal invasion → SVCO:** Tumor infiltrates the SVC wall, causing upstream venous congestion. This is an oncologic emergency.
- **Paraneoplastic syndromes:**
  - **SIADH (hyponatremia):** Tumor secretes ectopic ADH (vasopressin) – 15% of SCLC patients.
  - **Cushing's syndrome (ectopic ACTH):** Paraneoplastic overproduction of ACTH causes hypokalemia, hypertension, hyperglycemia. Rare but distinctive.
  - **LEMS (weakness, autonomic dysfunction):** Autoantibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels at neuromuscular junction. Improves with treatment? Yes, SCLC treatment can help.
- **Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis):** Apical (Pancoast) involvement destroys the sympathetic chain.
- **Rapid growth and early metastasis:** Angiogenesis and hematogenous spread. Common sites: brain (30% at diagnosis – that's why MRI brain is routine), liver, bone, adrenal glands.

**Connect to Pattern Recognition:**
The classic vignette combines local effects (cough, SVCO) with paraneoplastic (none in that vignette, but add LEMS if legs weak) and rapid progression (6 weeks, not months). The atypical presentations (LEMS, SIADH) are direct consequences of the neuroendocrine nature.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause / Trigger | 1-line stem clue that points to it |
|----------|-----------------|------------------------------------|
| **Environmental** | Cigarette smoking (90+%) | "Heavy smoker, 40+ pack-years" |
| | Occupational exposure (asbestos, radon, diesel exhaust) | "Worked in shipyard / uranium mine" |
| | Second-hand smoke (rare, but relevant) | "Non-smoker, but husband smoked" |
| **Idiopathic** | Unknown – SCLC in never-smoker without known exposure | "No smoking history, no exposures" – very rare; consider genetic predisposition (e.g., TP53 germline mutation) |
| **Immunosuppression** | HIV / organ transplant (increased risk of all lung cancers) | "On tacrolimus / HIV positive" |
| **Genetic** | Family history – first-degree relative with lung cancer | "Father died of lung cancer aged 50" |

**Most common cause:** Cigarette smoking. Over 90% of SCLC cases occur in smokers. This is the strongest risk factor for any lung cancer. Document pack-years.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Steps (with staging):**
1. **Chest X-ray (CXR)** – Often first clue. Shows hilar mass, mediastinal widening, maybe atelectasis.
   - *What it shows:* Central opacity.
   - *Why order:* Cheap, fast, but limited.
2. **CT chest + abdomen (with contrast)** – Defines primary tumor, mediastinal nodes, distant metastases (liver, adrenal).
   - *What it shows:* Enhancing central mass, often with bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
   - *Why:* Staging – we need to know limited vs. extensive stage.
3. **Biopsy** – Needle aspiration or bronchoscopy with endobronchial biopsy.
   - *What it shows:* Small blue cells, scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, high mitotic rate.
   - *Immunohistochemistry:* Synaptophysin +, chromogranin +, TTF-1 +, CD56 +, Ki-67 >50%.
   - *Why:* Confirm diagnosis and distinguish from NSCLC, lymphoma, carcinoid.
4. **MRI brain (with contrast)** – Mandatory for all SCLC (even if asymptomatic) due to high brain metastasis risk.
   - *What it shows:* Enhancing brain lesions – often multiple.
5. **PET-CT (or bone scan + CT)** – For staging when limited stage suspected.
   - *What it shows:* FDG-avid primary, nodes, metastases.
6. **Bloods:**
   - **FBC, LFT, LDH** – LDH is a prognostic marker (high LDH → worse prognosis).
   - **Serum sodium** – Check for SIADH. Normal is 135-145; <130 is moderate; <120 is severe.
   - **Cortisol / ACTH** if suspect ectopic Cushing.
7. **Pulmonary function tests (PFTs)** – Before treatment planning, especially if curative-intent chemoradiotherapy.

**Pattern Recognition – Single Finding That Confirms/Rules Out:**
- *Confirms:* Biopsy + neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin positive, TTF-1 positive) + high Ki-67.
- *Rules out:* If biopsy shows gland formation, squamous pearls – think NSCLC. If cells are large with prominent nucleoli – think large cell carcinoma. If lymphocyte-rich and low Ki-67 – think carcinoid.

**Staging System:**
SCLC uses a two-tier system (not TNM routinely): **Limited stage (LS)** = tumor confined to one hemithorax + ipsilateral mediastinal/hilar nodes, can be encompassed by a single radiotherapy port. **Extensive stage (ES)** = disease beyond that (contralateral lung, pleural effusion, distant metastases). About 30% present limited, 70% extensive.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – Oncologic Emergencies:**
- **Superior vena cava obstruction:** Elevate head, IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat (reduce edema), call oncology for urgent radiotherapy or stenting. Do not delay biopsy – it's safe.
- **Spinal cord compression:** Dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat, urgent MRI spine, consult neurosurgery / oncology. Radiotherapy 2-3 fractions.
- **Severe hyponatremia (SIADH):** Sodium <120 with seizures → hypertonic saline (3%) slow infusion (1-2 mL/kg over 1-2h), aim for 4-6 mmol rise. Furosemide if fluid overloaded. Then switch to tolvaptan (oral V2 antagonist) or demeclocycline.
- **Brain metastases with raised ICP:** Dexamethasone 8-16 mg IV daily, urgent whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) or stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) if few lesions.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – Definitive Treatment Planning:**
- **Limited stage (LS-SCLC):**
  - Concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CRT) – platinum + etoposide (cisplatin 75 mg/m² IV day 1 + etoposide 100 mg/m² IV days 1-3, q21d x4 cycles) + thoracic radiotherapy (≥1.5 Gy b.i.d. to total 45 Gy, or 66 Gy once daily). Start ASAP.
  - Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) – after CRT, if good response. Reduces brain mets risk but may impair cognition.
- **Extensive stage (ES-SCLC):**
  - Chemotherapy: carboplatin (AUC 5-6 IV day 1) + etoposide (100 mg/m² IV days 1-3) q21d x4-6 cycles.
  - Immunotherapy: Add atezolizumab (1200 mg IV q3w) or durvalumab (1500 mg IV q3w) – now standard for ES-SCLC. Maintenance with immune checkpoint inhibitor after chemo.
  - PCI not routinely recommended for ES-SCLC due to lack of survival benefit.

**Long-term:**
- Follow-up with CT chest every 3-6 months for 2 years, then annually.
- Manage treatment side effects: neutropenic fever (G-CSF), esophagitis (radiotherapy), fatigue, nausea.
- Palliative care: for progressive disease, second-line chemotherapy (topotecan, lurbinectedin) or clinical trials.
- Smoking cessation – improves outcomes and reduces second primary cancers.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT delay biopsy for SVCO – stenting/RT can proceed post-biopsy.
- Do NOT give whole-brain RT for 1-2 small brain mets – use SRS if possible.
- Do NOT use anti-VEGF (bevacizumab) in SCLC – increased risk of hemorrhage and no proven benefit.
- Do NOT forget PCI in LS-SCLC with complete response – it saves lives.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Small Cell Lung Cancer | Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) | Carcinoid Tumor | Lymphoma (Mediastinal) |
|---------|-----------------------|------------------------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Smoking** | Strong link (90%+ smokers) | Modest link (adenocarcinoma can be in never-smokers) | Weak/no link | None |
| **Growth rate** | Rapid (weeks) | Slow to moderate (months) | Very slow (years) | Moderate (weeks-months) |
| **Paraneoplastic syndromes** | Very common (SIADH, LEMS, ACTH) | Less common (hypercalcemia, HOA) | Rare (carcinoid syndrome if liver mets – very rare for lung) | Rare (fever, night sweats, pruritus) |
| **Central vs peripheral** | Central (major bronchi) | Variable (squamous: central; adeno: peripheral) | Central (usually) | Mediastinal (anterior) |
| **Biopsy histology** | Small blue cells, scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding | Large cells, gland formation/squamous pearls | Organoid nests, low mitotic rate | Lymphocytes with germinal centers |
| **Immunohistochemistry** | Synaptophysin +, chromogranin +, TTF-1 +, Ki-67 >50% | Cytokeratin +, p40 (squamous), TTF-1 (adeno) | Synaptophysin +, chromogranin +, Ki-67 <20% | CD45 +, B-cell or T-cell markers |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Rapid progression + paraneoplastic + heavy smoking | Larger tumor, no paraneoplastic, slower course | Incidental finding, no smoking, indolent | B symptoms (fever, weight loss) + bulky mediastinal nodes |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major Complications:**

1. **Superior Vena Cava Obstruction (SVCO)** – Presents with facial/neck swelling, dyspnea, distended neck veins, stridor. Key signs: Pemberton's sign (facial congestion on raising arms). Action: urgent dexamethasone + radiotherapy/stent. If missed: airway compromise, cerebral edema, death.
2. **Spinal Cord Compression** – Present with back pain (often first), then leg weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction. Key signs: percussion tenderness over vertebra. Action: high-dose dexamethasone, MRI urgent, radiotherapy or surgical decompression. If missed: permanent paraplegia.
3. **Hyponatremia (SIADH)** – Nausea, confusion, seizures, coma. Key signs: euvolemic hyponatremia, high urine osmolality (>100 mOsm/kg), low serum osmolality. Action: fluid restriction, 3% saline if severe, tolvaptan. If untreated: osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis) if corrected too fast.
4. **Brain Metastases** – Headache, focal deficits, seizure, cognitive decline. Key signs: papill edema on fundoscopy. Action: MRI brain, corticosteroids, WBRT or SRS. If missed: herniation, death.
5. **Venous Thromboembolism (VTE)** – SCLC is highly prothrombotic. DVT/PE can be first presentation. Action: anticoagulation (low molecular weight heparin preferred). If missed: fatal PE.
6. **Radiation Esophagitis** – After CRT: painful swallowing, odynophagia. Key sign: dysphagia. Action: oral analgesics, liquid diet, PPI. If severe: NG tube.
7. **Neutropenic Sepsis** – Post-chemotherapy: fever, rigors, source often unknown. Action: urgent blood cultures, IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h), G-CSF.

**Late Presentation Patterns (unmissable):**
"The patient presents with bilateral leg weakness and urinary retention. He has a 50 pack-year history. Chest X-ray shows a hilar mass. What is the most likely cause?" -> Spinal cord compression from SCLC metastasis.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **"SMALL CELL" for presentation clues:**
   - **S**moking history (almost always)
   - **M**ediastinal mass (central)
   - **A**rrhythmias? no – but **A**utonomic symptoms (LEMS: constipation, dry mouth)
   - **L**abial swelling (facial/SVCO)
   - **L**imp limb (brain mets or cord compression)
   - **C**ough, hemoptysis
   - **E**ndocrine (SIADH, ACTH)
   - **L**ow sodium
   - **L**DH elevated

2. **"EDGE" for staging:**
   - **E**ncompassable in one radiotherapy port? (Limited stage)
   - **D**istant metastasis? (Extensive stage)
   - **G**rade: high (Ki-67 >50%)
   - **E**mboli? (VTE risk)

3. **"VIP" for treatment drugs (historical, but memorable):**
   - **V**P-16 (etoposide)
   - **I**phosphamide (second-line)
   - **P**latinum (cisplatin/carboplatin)

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls (Pattern Recognition Cues):**

1. **Think SCLC first in a heavy smoker with a central lung mass and any paraneoplastic syndrome.** The differential is narrow.
2. **Hyponatremia in a smoker?** Check chest CT and urine osmolality. SCLC causes SIADH in 15%. Don't blame other causes until proven.
3. **Proximal muscle weakness + 'getting better' with repeated movement?** Think Lambert-Eaton, not myasthenia. EMG shows incrementing response. Anti-VGCC antibodies.
4. **Mediastinal widening on CXR?** SCLC, lymphoma, or sarcoid. The smoking history points to SCLC.
5. **Single brain lesion in a smoker?** Almost always SCLC metastasis. Lesion likely contrast-enhancing, peritumoral edema.
6. **Pitting edema of the face?** That's SVCO. Test for distended neck veins. It's an emergency.
7. **Rapidly growing mass in a smoker: SCLC doubles size in <30 days.** If you see a mass that's grown from 2 to 4 cm in a month, it's SCLC until proven otherwise.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**
- **Calling it NSCLC on small biopsy** – always stain for neuroendocrine markers in poorly differentiated lung cancer.
- **Not ordering an MRI brain** – SCLC metastasizes to brain early. MRI is part of staging.
- **Using CT alone for mediastinal staging** – PET-CT or endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) is better.
- **Withholding prophylactic cranial irradiation for fear of cognitive decline** – in LS-SCLC with good response, PCI reduces brain mets and improves survival.
- **Giving whole-brain RT for single small metastasis** – consider stereotactic radiosurgery first.
- **Underestimating the urgency** – SCLC is fast. Start workup ASAP.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (Registrar / Consultant):**
- **SVCO with stridor or respiratory distress** – risk of airway compromise. Call oncology, consider stenting. Start dexamethasone.
- **SiADH with seizures or GCS <15** – call ITU, start 3% saline.
- **Spinal cord compression** – call neurosurgery and oncology for urgent radiotherapy.
- **New neurological symptoms (headache, focal deficits)** – arrange urgent CT head / MRI brain.
- **Febrile neutropenia** – if neutrophils <1.0 and temp >38°C, call oncology and start IV antibiotics. Consider sepsis protocol.
- **Massive hemoptysis** – call for rigid bronchoscopy / embolization.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (or any single parameter score 3) – escalate to critical care outreach.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** – suggests tissue hypoperfusion; ICU review.
- **GCS ≤ 12** (or drop by ≥2) – call anaesthetics for airway assessment, CT head.
- **Sodium <125 mmol/L** (or <120 with symptoms) – consider ITU admission for controlled correction.
- **Platelets <20,000/mcL** or **active bleeding** – call haematology and transfuse.
- **Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) not responding to fluids** – sepsis or PE? Call ICU.

**When to call crash team:** Cardiorespiratory arrest, witnessed seizure with loss of airway, unresponsive patient with agonal breathing.
